Eating — a necessity of biological life — is among the most ordinary of acts, yet the Islamic tradition has surrounded it with a rich set of etiquettes (adab) that transform a mundane need into an opportunity for remembrance of Allah, gratitude, and the cultivation of good character. These etiquettes are documented in authentic hadith and were consistently practiced by the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) and his companions.

The Prophet (peace be upon him) said: "A believer eats in one intestine while a disbeliever eats in seven" (Bukhari and Muslim). This hadith does not literally mean that believers and disbelievers have different digestive anatomy. Scholars have interpreted it as indicating that the believer eats with moderation — with one portion of the stomach, with gratitude, aware of who gave the food — while the one heedless of Allah eats without restraint or awareness. Moderation and mindfulness are the foundation of Islamic eating etiquette.

Before Eating

The etiquettes before eating begin with washing the hands — a practice of cleanliness that predates the germ theory of disease by many centuries. The Prophet (peace be upon him) commanded it and practiced it consistently. The meal should be placed on a cloth or mat on the floor or ground, following the prophetic sunnah, rather than elevated platforms that might encourage a sense of pride. However, scholars have noted that this specific manner is a preferred sunnah, not an obligation, and Muslims in different cultures have adopted local customs for presenting food without contradiction.

The essential verbal etiquette before eating is saying Bismillah — "In the name of Allah." The Prophet (peace be upon him) explicitly commanded this: "When any of you eats, let him mention the name of Allah. If he forgets to mention the name of Allah at the beginning, let him say: Bismillahi awwalahu wa akhirahu — in the name of Allah at its beginning and its end" (Abu Dawud, Tirmidhi). If one forgets, this formula said at any point during the meal counts.

During the Meal

The Prophet (peace be upon him) ate with his right hand and instructed Muslims to do the same: "Eat with your right hand and drink with your right hand, for Shaytan eats with his left hand and drinks with his left hand" (Muslim). This is a sunnah mu'akkadah — an emphasized prophetic practice — that Muslims should observe. Those with physical impairment or who cannot use their right hand for a legitimate reason are not held to this requirement.

The Prophet also instructed eating from what is nearest to oneself on the shared dish, not reaching across to take from another part of the platter. He corrected a young Umar ibn Abi Salamah who was reaching across the dish: "Say Bismillah, eat with your right hand, and eat from what is near to you" (Bukhari and Muslim). This guidance is both hygienic and courteous.

Standing while eating is discouraged based on authentic hadith, though scholars note that it is permissible in cases of necessity. Eating while reclining on one's side is also discouraged, based on the Prophet's statement that he did not eat while reclining (Bukhari). The recommended posture is sitting upright — modest, alert, and present.

Using fingers is the prophetic sunnah, and the Prophet (peace be upon him) specifically recommended licking the fingers after eating rather than wiping them on a cloth — because "he does not know in which part of the food the blessing is" (Muslim). This is a remarkable integration of practical frugality and spiritual awareness: every part of the provision is valuable, and none of it should be wasted carelessly.

After Eating

After finishing a meal, the Prophet (peace be upon him) would praise Allah. The authenticated du'a after eating is: "Alhamdulillahi hamdan kathiran tayyiban mubarakan fihi ghayra makfiyin wa la muwadda'in wa la mustaghnan 'anhu Rabbuna" — praise be to Allah, much good and blessed praise, not enough, not bid farewell, and not in need of being dispensed with, O our Lord (Bukhari). A shorter common practice is simply: "Alhamdulillah alladhi at'amana wa saqana wa ja'alana min al-muslimin."

After the meal, the hands are washed again. The guest should make du'a for the host who provided food, as the Prophet (peace be upon him) taught. Communal eating — sharing food with family, guests, neighbors, and the poor — is a highly recommended practice throughout Islamic tradition. The Prophet said that food for two is enough for three, and food for three is enough for four. Hospitality and generosity with food are among the most praised characteristics in Islamic ethics.
morning-evening-adhkar:
Fortifying the Day with Remembrance

The Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) described a Muslim who remembers Allah and one who does not as being like the living and the dead (Bukhari). This comparison is not rhetorical excess — it reflects a profound truth: the heart without dhikr loses its orientation, its vitality, and its connection to the source of all good. The morning and evening adhkar (plural of dhikr, remembrance) are the prophetically prescribed practices that anchor a Muslim's day in awareness of Allah.

The Quran commands this practice explicitly: "And remember your Lord much and exalt Him in the evening and in the morning" (Quran 3:41). "So be patient over what they say and exalt Allah with praise of your Lord before the rising of the sun and before its setting" (Quran 50:39). The Quran also promises specific protection for those who maintain this practice: "Indeed, good deeds do away with misdeeds" (Quran 11:114). The morning and evening adhkar are among those good deeds.

The Morning Adhkar

The morning adhkar are recited after fajr prayer, ideally while sitting in one's place of prayer until the sun has fully risen. They include a comprehensive set of formulas that cover spiritual protection, gratitude, affirmation of faith, and supplication for good in the day ahead.

Among the most important is Sayyid al-Istighfar — the Master of Seeking Forgiveness. The Prophet (peace be upon him) said: "The master of seeking forgiveness is to say: O Allah, You are my Lord. There is no deity but You. You created me and I am Your servant. I am following Your covenant and promise as best I can. I seek refuge in You from the evil of what I have done. I acknowledge Your blessing upon me and I confess my sin to You, so forgive me, for none forgives sins but You." He then said: "Whoever says this with conviction in the morning and dies that day before evening will be among the people of Paradise" (Bukhari).

The morning adhkar also include Ayat al-Kursi (2:255) — the Throne Verse — which the Prophet (peace be upon him) described as a protection such that "nothing will prevent him from entering Paradise except death" if recited after each obligatory prayer (Nasa'i, Ibn Hibban). Surah al-Ikhlas, al-Falaq, and al-Nas are recited three times each in the morning and evening, providing comprehensive protection from all harm, whether spiritual, physical, or from the unseen.

The Evening Adhkar

The evening adhkar are recited after Asr prayer and before sunset, or in the early part of the evening. They mirror and complement the morning set, completing the frame of protection around the Muslim's day. The formula for evening differs slightly in wording from the morning: where morning says "asbahna" (we have reached the morning), evening says "amsayna" (we have reached the evening).

The Prophet (peace be upon him) recommended: "Say: 'We have reached the evening and the kingdom belongs to Allah. All praise is for Allah. There is no deity but Allah, alone, without partner. To Him belongs the kingdom and to Him belongs all praise, and He is over all things competent.' Whoever says this in the evening will be protected until the morning; and whoever says this in the morning will be protected until the evening" (Abu Dawud). This formula of 100 repetitions of la ilaha illa Allah and similar formulas are also recommended.

The Comprehensive Supplication: Du'a for Protection

Among the most beloved morning and evening formulas is: "Bismillahi alladhi la yadurru ma'a ismihi shay'un fi al-ardi wa la fi al-sama'i wa huwa al-sami'u al-'alim" — In the name of Allah with whose name nothing on earth or in heaven can cause harm, and He is the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing. The Prophet (peace be upon him) promised that whoever says this three times in the morning and three times in the evening will not be harmed by anything (Abu Dawud, Tirmidhi — hasan sahih).

How to Practice

The morning and evening adhkar can be learned progressively — beginning with the most essential formulas and adding more as they become familiar and internalized. Small booklets compiling the prophetically authenticated adhkar (such as Hisnul Muslim — Fortress of the Muslim, compiled by Sa'id al-Qahtani) provide an organized collection. The key is consistency over volume: a brief daily practice sustained over years is more beneficial than occasional comprehensive sessions followed by long gaps.

The Prophet (peace be upon him) said: "The most beloved deeds to Allah are those done consistently, even if they are small" (Bukhari). Morning and evening adhkar, practiced daily, build the spiritual foundation on which everything else rests — a heart that begins each day in remembrance of Allah and ends each day in gratitude to Him.
muhasabah-self-accounting:
Umar ibn al-Khattab, the second Caliph of Islam and one of the most perceptive spiritual minds among the Companions, delivered a counsel that has echoed through every generation of Islamic scholarship: "Account yourselves before you are accounted. Weigh your deeds before they are weighed. And prepare for the Greatest Presentation." The Day of Judgment will be a day of precise accounting — not a single deed, word, or hidden intention will be missed. The believer who understands this prepares not by anxiety but by practice: the daily discipline of muhasabah, self-reckoning, turning the tools of accountability inward before they are turned outward by the Divine.

The Quranic Basis for Self-Accounting

Allah commands in the Quran: "O you who have believed, fear Allah. And let every soul look to what it has put forth for tomorrow — and fear Allah." (59:18). Ibn Kathir commented on this verse that "looking to what one has put forth" is precisely muhasabah: examining whether what one has sent ahead to the akhirah is worthy of what awaits there. The Prophet ﷺ reinforced this: "The intelligent person is the one who subdues his nafs and works for what comes after death. The incapable person is the one who follows his desires and hopes in Allah." (Tirmidhi). Intelligence, in the prophetic paradigm, is not academic — it is the capacity to see beyond the immediate and act in light of what is permanent.

Muhasabah Before and After Action

Scholars of spiritual discipline distinguished between two types of muhasabah. The first is muhasabah before the action: pausing to examine one's intention before speaking, deciding, or acting. Is this for Allah or for the approval of people? Will this benefit my akhirah or harm it? Al-Muhasibi, whose very name (al-muhasibi = "the one who accounts himself") reflects his legacy, wrote at length in al-Ri'ayah li-Huquq Allah about the need to inspect the intention before every action with the same rigor a merchant inspects his accounts. The second is muhasabah after the action: a daily review of what one did, said, and intended throughout the day. Where did I fall short? What did I get right? What do I owe — to Allah, to others, to my own soul?

The Daily Practice of the Salaf

The early Muslims were meticulous self-accountants. It is reported that Ibn Mas'ud would not let a day end without sitting with himself and asking: "What did I do today? For whom did I do it?" Hasan al-Basri said: "The believer is the guardian of his own soul — he accounts himself for the sake of Allah. The Day of Judgment will be light for those who accounted themselves in this world, and heavy for those who did not." This was not a spiritual practice of the elite alone — it was the ordinary habit of a generation that took the akhirah seriously as a destination they were actively traveling toward.

What to Account

A comprehensive daily muhasabah covers several domains. First: the obligatory duties — were the five prayers performed with presence and on time? Was the fast observed? Were rights fulfilled? Second: speech — did my tongue wander into backbiting, lies, or vain talk? The Prophet ﷺ said: "Most of what will bring people to the hellfire is the two openings: the mouth and the private parts." (Tirmidhi). Third: the heart — was there envy, rancor, pride, or attachment to the world in my heart today? These invisible sins are harder to catch than visible ones and require sharper inner sight. Fourth: intentions — were the good deeds I performed genuinely for Allah, or did the desire for recognition creep in?

The Spiritual Fruit of Muhasabah

The person who practices muhasabah consistently undergoes a transformation. Faults that were invisible become visible. Patterns of sin that seemed random reveal themselves as habits with identifiable triggers. Small mercies that went unnoticed become a source of gratitude and wonder. The believer who knows exactly where they fall short is far better positioned for improvement than the one who goes through life with a general sense of "I could be better" but no specifics. The Prophet ﷺ said: "The believer sees his sins as if he is sitting beneath a mountain afraid it will fall on him, while the sinner sees his sins like a fly that landed on his nose and he waves away." (Bukhari). Muhasabah is the practice that gives the believer that mountain-awareness — not to crush them, but to keep them humble, vigilant, and perpetually oriented toward Allah.
tazkiyah-purification-soul:
Among the eleven oaths Allah takes at the opening of Surah al-Shams — by the sun, the moon, the day, the night, the sky, the earth, and the soul — He concludes with one of the most decisive verdicts in the Quran: "Indeed, he who purifies it (the soul) has succeeded, and he who corrupts it has failed." (91:9-10). Success — falah, the comprehensive flourishing in this life and the next — is tied not to wealth, status, intelligence, or even knowledge. It is tied to the purification of the soul. This is tazkiyah al-nafs, the central project of the believer's inner life.

The Soul That Allah Created

The Quran describes the nafs as having been given both its capacity for corruption and its capacity for purity: "By the soul and He who proportioned it and inspired it with its wickedness and its righteousness." (91:7-8). The human being is not born evil — Islamic theology is clear that the fitrah (primordial nature) is pure. But the soul has appetites, inclinations, and vulnerabilities that can pull it toward what harms it. Ibn al-Qayyim described three stages of the nafs: the nafs al-ammarah bi-l-su' (the soul that commands toward evil, 12:53), the nafs al-lawwamah (the self-reproaching soul, 75:2), and the nafs al-mutmainnah (the soul at rest, 89:27). Tazkiyah is the journey from the first to the last.

The Diseases of the Heart

Classical scholars of the heart — among them al-Ghazali, Ibn al-Qayyim, and al-Muhasibi — catalogued the major spiritual diseases the believer must diagnose and treat. Kibr (arrogance): the sense of self-superiority that was the first sin (Iblis's refusal to bow). Hasad (envy): wishing for another's blessing to be removed, described by the Prophet ﷺ as eating away good deeds like fire consumes wood. Riya (ostentation): performing acts of worship for human approval — the Prophet ﷺ called it the "lesser shirk" (Ahmad). Hubb al-dunya (love of the world): described by the Prophet ﷺ as "the root of every evil." Ghaflah (heedlessness): living as though Allah is not watching and the akhirah is not approaching.

The Role of Worship in Purification

Worship is not merely an obligation discharged — it is the primary medicine of the heart. The Quran says that salah "prevents immorality and wrongdoing" (29:45). Fasting subdues the nafs al-ammarah. The Prophet ﷺ said: "Fasting is a shield." (Bukhari). Zakat and sadaqah cure attachment to wealth. And dhikr — the remembrance of Allah — is specifically identified as the medicine for the heedless heart: "Truly, in the remembrance of Allah do hearts find rest." (13:28). Al-Ghazali's Ihya Ulum al-Din organizes its entire second half around the cure of heart diseases through worship, dhikr, and reflection.

Practical Steps Toward Purification

Tazkiyah requires both knowledge and practice — one without the other is incomplete. The practical path includes: Tawbah — sincere repentance that wipes the slate, not once but continually. Muhasabah — daily self-accounting to catch the subtle creep of spiritual disease before it takes root. Muraqabah — the sustained awareness that Allah watches the heart, not just the outward deeds. Du'a — asking Allah directly for purity, following the model of the Prophet ﷺ who prayed: "O Allah, give my soul its taqwa and purify it — You are the best to purify it." (Muslim). And suhbah — companionship with the righteous, whose presence raises the spiritual atmosphere.

Tazkiyah Is a Prophetic Mission

Allah identified tazkiyah as one of the four central missions of the Prophet ﷺ: "He is the one who raised among the unlettered people a Messenger from themselves, reciting to them His verses and purifying them (yuzakkihim) and teaching them the Book and the wisdom." (62:2). Purification — not just instruction — was part of what the Prophet ﷺ was sent to do. This means that tazkiyah is not an optional pursuit for spiritual enthusiasts: it is the central work that prophethood came to accomplish. Every Muslim who follows the Prophet ﷺ is, by that very following, engaged in the project of purifying the soul.

The Goal: The Pure Heart

The Prophet ۷ said: “Indeed, in the body there is a piece of flesh — if it is good, the whole body is good; and if it is corrupt, the whole body is corrupt. Indeed, it is the heart.” (Bukhari, Muslim). Tazkiyah al-nafs is ultimately the purification of this piece of flesh — not the biological organ but the spiritual center from which all of human life flows. The Quran names the goal: the qalb saleem, the sound heart. “The day when neither wealth nor children will be of any benefit — only the one who comes to Allah with a sound heart.” (26:88-89). This is the destination. Every practice of tazkiyah, every prayer with presence, every act of dhikr, every moment of muhasabah, every choice to give when the nafs wants to withhold — all of it is the road toward that sound heart. The believer who dies with a qalb saleem has succeeded in the only way that matters.
hubb-al-dunya-love-of-world:
The Prophet ﷺ said: "Love of the world is the root of every evil." This hadith identifies a single cause at the root of the human being's greatest failures: attachment to the temporary over the permanent, the created over the Creator, the passing pleasure over the enduring reward. Understanding what hubb al-dunya actually means — and what it does not mean — is one of the most practically important matters in Islamic spiritual life.

What Hubb al-Dunya Means

The Arabic word dunya comes from a root meaning "near" or "low." The world is the near thing, the low thing — the present before the Absent, the immediate before the Ultimate. Loving the world does not simply mean enjoying a good meal, wearing beautiful clothes, or building a comfortable home. The Prophet ﷺ himself enjoyed honey, wore fine garments on occasions, and cared for his physical needs. What hubb al-dunya means is prioritizing the world over the akhirah — allowing the appetite for worldly gain to override obedience to Allah, willingness to commit haram for money or pleasure, and the state of the heart in which the akhirah becomes theoretical while the world becomes the practical present. Ibn al-Qayyim described it as the heart being filled with the world such that the akhirah has no room to settle.

The Quranic Warning

The Quran is relentless in describing the world's nature and warning against being deceived by it. "Know that the life of this world is but amusement and diversion and adornment and boasting to one another and competition in increase of wealth and children — like the example of a rain whose resulting plant growth pleases the tillers; then it dries and you see it turned yellow; then it becomes debris. And in the Hereafter is severe punishment and forgiveness from Allah and approval. And what is the worldly life except the enjoyment of delusion." (57:20). Allah does not say the world is evil — He says it is temporary and deceptive.

Zuhd: The Islamic Response

Zuhd — asceticism, or more precisely, detachment from the world — is the prophetic response to hubb al-dunya. The Prophet ﷺ said: "Be detached from the world (zuhd fi-l-dunya) and Allah will love you. Be detached from what people possess and people will love you." (Ibn Majah). Scholars distinguish between zuhd and poverty. Ahmad ibn Hanbal said zuhd is "not having the world in your heart." A wealthy person who uses their wealth for Allah and is not attached to it has more zuhd than a poor person who is consumed by wanting what they do not have. Sufyan al-Thawri said: "Zuhd in the world is having short hopes — it is not eating coarse food or wearing a rough garment."

The Symptoms and Consequences

Hubb al-dunya manifests in recognizable ways. The person afflicted delays salah for worldly concerns, is willing to compromise honesty in business for profit, feels more distress at financial loss than at sin, and spends more energy planning for retirement than for the akhirah. The Prophet ﷺ warned: "Two hungry wolves sent among a flock of sheep cause less damage to them than a man's eagerness for wealth and status causes to his religion." (Tirmidhi). The consequence is the hollowing out of spiritual life.

Using the World for the Akhirah

The balanced Islamic position is not rejection of the world but use of the world for the akhirah. The Quran says: "And seek, through that which Allah has given you, the home of the Hereafter; and do not forget your share of the world." (28:77). The world is a passage and a resource, not a destination. The Prophet ﷺ said: "What do I have to do with the world? I am in the world only like a rider who rests in the shade of a tree and then moves on." (Tirmidhi). This traveler-consciousness — living with an eye always on the destination — is the practical antidote to hubb al-dunya.

The Balance: Living in the World Without Being Owned by It

The prophetic model is not the hermit who flees society but the engaged believer who lives fully in the world while remaining internally free from it. The Companions were merchants, farmers, soldiers, and leaders — deeply involved in the affairs of the world — and yet the Prophet ۷ described them as people whose hearts were attached to the akhirah. Abu Bakr al-Siddiq was among the wealthiest in Mecca and gave every dirham he owned in the path of Allah. Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf was one of the most successful merchants in Medina and spent enormously in charity. Their wealth was in their hands, not in their hearts. The measure of one's relationship to the dunya is not what one owns but what owns one — whether the thought of losing it would make one compromise one's deen. The believer who can say honestly that losing their wealth would not cause them to abandon an obligation has achieved a form of zuhd that the one who owns nothing has yet to prove.
kibr-arrogance:
The first sin in the history of creation was not committed by a human being — it was committed by Iblis. And the nature of that sin was not theft, or violence, or even disbelief in God's existence. It was kibr — arrogance. When Allah commanded the angels to bow to Adam, Iblis refused: "He refused and was arrogant and became of the disbelievers." (2:34). The Quran's word for his refusal is astakbara — he deemed himself too great. This is kibr at its essence: considering oneself above what Allah has ordained, whether it is a divine command, a person, or a truth that humbles the ego.

The Prophetic Definition

The Prophet ﷺ defined kibr with precision when a Companion asked him about it: "Kibr is rejecting the truth and looking down upon people." (Muslim). This two-part definition is incisive. The first dimension — rejecting the truth — explains why kibr is so spiritually devastating. A person without arrogance who encounters a correct argument accepts it, even if it means admitting they were wrong. A person with kibr cannot accept being wrong, so they dismiss evidence and stick to error rather than face the diminishment of their self-image. The second dimension — looking down upon people — produces injustice in relationships.

The Hadith of the Atom's Weight

The severity of kibr in the prophetic tradition is staggering: "He who has an atom's weight of kibr in his heart will not enter Paradise." (Muslim). When a Companion expressed concern — "But a man likes to have nice clothes and nice sandals" — the Prophet ﷺ clarified: "Allah is Beautiful and loves beauty. Kibr is rejecting the truth and looking down upon people." (Muslim). Enjoying fine clothes is not kibr. Taking pride in a genuine achievement is not kibr. What is prohibited is the internal posture of self-superiority that leads to dismissing the truth and degrading other people.

Manifestations of Kibr

Kibr takes many forms, some obvious and some subtle. Obvious manifestations include refusing to greet those of lower social standing, being unable to accept advice or criticism, speaking over others in conversation, and expecting to be served without reciprocating. Subtler forms include taking pleasure in others' failures, feeling resentment when others are praised, and the spiritual kibr of believing one's worship or knowledge makes one superior to those who practice less. Ibn al-Qayyim noted that spiritual kibr — the arrogance of the devotee who looks down on the sinner — is among the most dangerous forms because it wears the clothing of piety.

Kibr Versus al-'Izzah

It is important to distinguish kibr from the legitimate honor and dignity that Islam affirms. Al-'izzah — the quality of dignity and self-respect — is praised in the Quran: "Honor belongs to Allah, to His Messenger, and to the believers." (63:8). Standing firm for the truth, refusing to compromise one's principles, and maintaining dignity in the face of those who would demean the faith is not kibr — it is 'izzah. The distinction is the direction: 'izzah is directed upward, toward Allah and His truth. Kibr is directed downward, toward other people whom one looks upon as lesser.

The Cure for Kibr

Several cures for arrogance are prescribed by scholars. First is tafakkur — contemplation of one's origins and end. The Quran repeatedly invites reflection: "Did We not create you from a liquid disdained?" (77:20). From a drop of fluid to a handful of dust — the beginning and end of the human being are humbling. Second is seeking knowledge of one's own faults. Third is serving others — deliberately placing oneself in roles of service, especially to those the ego considers beneath it. The Prophet ﷺ used to sit with the poor, help with household tasks, and refuse to distinguish himself by special treatment. Fourth is du'a — asking Allah for humility, since the heart is between Allah's fingers and He turns it as He wills.

Tawadu: The Opposite of Kibr

Just as kibr is the quality that bars entry to Paradise, its opposite — tawadu' (humility) — is among the most beloved qualities to Allah. The Prophet ۷ said: “Whoever humbles himself before Allah, Allah raises him; and whoever raises himself above others, Allah humbles him.” (Ahmad, authenticated). The person of tawadu' does not seek recognition, does not demand preferential treatment, is willing to learn from those younger or less educated, and does not differentiate between people based on social status. True humility is not self-deprecation — it is an accurate perception of oneself in relation to Allah and to the created order. The person who knows what they owe to Allah and what they genuinely carry of faults has no basis for kibr. The Prophet ۷ walked among his Companions as if he were one of them — sitting on the ground, milking his own goat, sewing his own sandals. This was tawadu' in practice, and it is the antidote to every form of kibr.
hasad-envy:
Among the diseases of the heart that destroy spiritual life from within, hasad — envy — occupies a uniquely dangerous position. It is the only sin that provides no pleasure to the sinner: the one who is envious gains nothing by their envy except suffering. Yet the Prophet ﷺ warned against it with singular urgency: "Beware of envy, for verily hasad eats the good deeds as fire eats wood." (Abu Dawud). The image is precise. A person may spend years accumulating a wealth of righteous deeds — prayers, fasts, charity, dhikr — and hasad can devour it all, not suddenly but steadily, the way embers consume a log.

What Hasad Is — and What It Is Not

The Arabic word hasad means to wish for the removal of a blessing from another person. This is its technical definition: not merely wishing you had what someone else has, but wishing that they did not have it. This distinction separates hasad from the closely related — and permissible — quality of ghibtah. Ghibtah is when you see a blessing someone else has and you wish Allah would give you the same, without wishing it to be removed from them. The Prophet ﷺ allowed ghibtah in two specific cases: "There is no envy (ghibtah) except in two cases: a man to whom Allah gives wealth and he spends it in the cause of truth, and a man to whom Allah gives wisdom and he judges by it and teaches it." (Bukhari, Muslim).

The Quranic Context

The Quran references hasad in multiple contexts. The story of the sons of Adam is at its root a story of hasad — when Qabil's sacrifice was not accepted and Habil's was, Qabil's envy led to the first murder in human history. The Quran describes the People of the Book's reaction to the revelation given to the Prophet ﷺ: "Many of the People of the Scripture wish they could turn you back to disbelief after you have believed, out of envy from themselves." (2:109). And in Surah al-Falaq, among the evils from which the believer seeks Allah's refuge is "the evil of the envier when he envies." (113:5).

The Spiritual Damage of Hasad

Beyond its destruction of good deeds, hasad damages the believer in several interlocking ways. It produces constant suffering — the envious person is pained every time they see the object of their envy succeed. It generates grudges and resentment that corrode relationships. It leads to backbiting — one who envies another is naturally drawn to speaking ill of them. And it represents an implicit objection to Allah's distribution of His blessings. The one who envies is, at some level, dissatisfied with how Allah arranged His creation — a spiritual posture that undermines the contentment that Islam seeks to cultivate.

The Cures for Hasad

The scholars of the heart prescribed several remedies. The first is to recognize hasad as a disease of the self and turn the resentment into du'a: praying for the person one envies. The Prophet ﷺ said: "None of you truly believes until he loves for his brother what he loves for himself." (Bukhari, Muslim). Praying for the one you envy is practicing this love in its most demanding form. The second remedy is gratitude — deeply engaging with the blessings Allah has given oneself rather than fixating on the blessings given to others. The Quran says: "And if you should count the favors of Allah, you could not enumerate them." (14:34). Third is contemplating the wisdom behind Allah's differentiated distribution of blessings — that what looks like a blessing to the outside observer may be a trial for the recipient.

Protecting Oneself from the Envy of Others

Islam also addresses the believer as a potential target of others' envy. The evil eye (al-ayn) — which is caused by excessive admiration or envy directed at another — is confirmed in the Quran and hadith. The Prophet ۷ said: “The evil eye is real, and if anything were to overtake the divine decree, the evil eye would do so.” (Muslim). Protection comes through reciting the refuge surahs (al-Falaq and al-Nas), through ruqyah (prescribed supplication), and through the common practice of saying Masha Allah la quwwata illa billah when admiring something belonging to oneself or another. The wise believer is grateful in private, does not flaunt what Allah has given, and relies on Allah's protection against the harm of those who may envy their blessings. The combination of inner protection (curing one's own hasad) and outer protection (du'a and ruqyah) gives the believer a comprehensive defense against one of the most widespread spiritual harms.
wara-scrupulousness:
In one of the most foundational statements of Islamic jurisprudence and spirituality, the Prophet ﷺ said: "The halal is clear and the haram is clear, and between them are doubtful matters that many people do not know about. So whoever avoids doubtful matters has preserved his religion and his honor, and whoever falls into doubtful matters falls into the haram, like the shepherd who grazes his flock near a protected preserve — he is liable to encroach upon it. Indeed, every king has a protected preserve, and Allah's preserve is His prohibitions." (Bukhari, Muslim). This hadith is the foundation of wara' — the spiritual station of scrupulousness, of deliberately keeping a safe distance from the prohibited by also avoiding what lies adjacent to it.

Wara' as a Spiritual Station

Classical scholars of spiritual discipline ranked wara' among the highest stations of the path to Allah. Al-Junayd said: "Wara' is leaving every doubtful thing." Ibn al-Qayyim described wara' as the fruit of taqwa: a person who truly fears Allah will not merely avoid the explicit prohibitions but will maintain a margin of safety around them, recognizing that the nafs is deceptive and the shaytan is subtle. The difference between the ordinary believer and the person of wara' is not just in what they avoid, but in how carefully they monitor the boundary — the scrupulous person is always asking not merely "Is this allowed?" but "Should I?"

The Practice of the Salaf

The early Muslims were famous for their scrupulousness in ways that strike modern observers as extraordinary. Umar ibn al-Khattab once found a dirham at home he could not account for and donated it to charity rather than spend it on himself. Abu Bakr al-Siddiq, when his servant brought him food and he learned it might have been improperly obtained, vomited it up — saying: "Even if the food came to the top of my throat, I would force it out, for the Prophet ﷺ said: 'Every body nourished by the haram, the fire is more deserving of it.'" Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal refused to eat certain foods he could not verify the source of. These practices reflect the depth of consciousness that comes when the akhirah is more real to a person than the dunya.

Wara' in Speech

Wara' is not only about food and financial dealings — it extends with equal importance to speech. The Prophet ﷺ said: "Whoever believes in Allah and the Last Day, let him speak good or remain silent." (Bukhari, Muslim). The person of wara' guards their tongue with the same vigilance they guard their income. Before speaking, they pause to ask: Is this true? Is it necessary? Is it kind? This is not timidity — it is the deliberate restraint of speech that would be vain, harmful, or uncertain. Sufyan al-Thawri said: "I have not seen anything easier to guard than the tongue, yet I see nothing more neglected."

Wara' in Financial Dealings

The application of wara' to money is where it is most practically tested in the modern world. Riba (interest) is unambiguously haram, but around it are many financial products that involve some of its elements in complex arrangements — the person of wara' avoids not just the clear riba but the gray zone that surrounds it. The hadith of the shepherd is apt: the one who grazes right at the edge of the preserve will eventually enter it.

The Fruit of Wara'

The Prophet ﷺ gave a remarkable promise to those who practice wara': "Be scrupulous about what is forbidden and you will be the most devoted of people to worship. Be content with what Allah has allotted you and you will be the wealthiest of people." (Tirmidhi). The connection between wara' and devotion is profound: a person whose income, food, and dealings are pure experiences a lightness in worship that the one entangled in doubtful matters does not. The heart that is not burdened by the weight of unresolved spiritual compromise can reach toward Allah with a freedom that the entangled heart cannot. Wara', in this sense, is not just an ethical standard — it is the prerequisite for a certain quality of spiritual life.

In our time, when commercial, digital, and social life offer an unprecedented number of ambiguous transactions and interactions, the practice of wara' requires conscious and ongoing effort. The believer who cultivates wara' is not paralyzed by uncertainty — they make principled decisions, seek knowledge to clarify what was unclear, and maintain an inner orientation of caution that prevents the gradual drift toward the haram that characterizes those who never think about boundaries. This orientation, sustained over a lifetime, produces the clarity of conscience that allows the dying person to meet Allah without the burden of unresolved spiritual compromise. Wara' is ultimately not a list of prohibitions but a way of moving through the world — attentively, reverently, with the shepherd's instinct always to keep the flock well away from the boundary.
husn-al-zann-positive-thinking:
The Prophet ﷺ reported Allah as saying in a hadith qudsi: "I am as My servant thinks of Me." (Bukhari, Muslim). This statement is among the most important in all of hadith literature because it reveals a direct connection between the believer's inner conception of Allah and what they actually receive from Him. The one who approaches Allah expecting mercy, forgiveness, and relief will find it. This is husn al-zann billah — having good thoughts, high expectations, and a positive orientation toward Allah — and it is not optional optimism but a religious obligation.

The Dying Moment

The importance of husn al-zann is most pronounced at the moment of death. The Prophet ﷺ said: "Let none of you die except while having a good opinion of Allah." (Muslim). In another narration: "Let none of you die except while having a good opinion of Allah, for indeed I heard Allah saying: 'I am as My servant thinks of Me — if he thinks good of Me, I will give him good, and if he thinks evil of Me, I will give him evil.'" (Ahmad). The final moments of life, when the soul is departing and the veil is thinning, are the moments when the quality of one's relationship with Allah becomes most visible. The believer who has cultivated husn al-zann throughout their life arrives at that moment with a heart that naturally inclines to hope.

The Theological Basis

Husn al-zann billah is grounded in the very nature of Allah as revealed in the Quran. Among His names are al-Rahman al-Rahim — the Supremely Merciful, the Continuously Merciful. The Prophet ﷺ told us that Allah has one hundred portions of mercy: He sent down one portion to the world, and it is by that one portion that all created beings show compassion to each other. The other ninety-nine He has kept for the Day of Judgment. "Allah is more merciful to His servants than this mother is to her child." (Bukhari, Muslim). This is the Allah about Whom one should have good thoughts. The Quran says: "My mercy encompasses all things." (7:156).

Husn al-Zann in Hardship

The test of husn al-zann comes not in times of ease but in hardship — when the prayer is not answered, when illness persists, when loss comes and does not leave. The Prophet ﷺ said: "Wondrous is the affair of the believer — all of his affairs are good. If good befalls him, he is grateful, and that is good for him. If hardship befalls him, he is patient, and that is good for him." (Muslim). This wondrous state is only possible if one has husn al-zann billah: the deep conviction that whatever Allah wills is, in some real sense, good for the believer — even if the good is not immediately visible.

Husn al-Zann Toward People

Husn al-zann also applies to one's fellow human beings. The Quran warns: "O you who have believed, avoid much negative assumption (zann). Indeed, some assumption is sin." (49:12). Assuming the worst about people without evidence and interpreting ambiguous actions in the most negative possible light are forms of su' al-zann that the Quran explicitly prohibits. Umar ibn al-Khattab said: "Do not think ill of a word spoken by your brother when you can find a good interpretation for it."

Cultivating Husn al-Zann

Husn al-zann is built through knowledge of Allah's names and attributes: the more deeply one knows al-Ghafur, al-Rahim, al-Tawwab, al-Latif, the more naturally one expects mercy and good from Him. It is built through reflection on personal experience: counting the specific moments when Allah answered a du'a, removed a hardship, or brought an unexpected good. And it is built through the stories of the prophets and righteous who experienced loss and difficulty and emerged — through patience and trust — with a faith that was stronger, not weaker. Husn al-zann is ultimately the posture of the servant who has truly believed in what Allah has said about Himself.

A Practical Framework

Practically, husn al-zann billah can be cultivated through three disciplines. First, study of the divine attributes: spending time each day reflecting on one of Allah's names — al-Ghafur (the Forgiving), al-Wadud (the Loving), al-Haleem (the Forbearing) — and allowing the reality of that name to settle in the heart. Second, counting blessings: the Quran connects the counting of blessings to the recognition that they are from Allah alone (16:53). The person who counts what they have received from Allah builds an experiential archive of divine generosity that sustains husn al-zann even when circumstances are dark. Third, reading the accounts of those who trusted Allah in hardship and were not disappointed: Ibrahim cast into fire that became cool, Yunus in the belly of the whale who was answered, Maryam giving birth alone who found sustenance where she expected none. These accounts are evidence for the believing heart of what Allah does when His servant trusts Him.
rights-of-neighbors-article:
Among the sustained themes of prophetic emphasis, few are more persistent than the rights of the neighbor. Abdullah ibn Umar reported: "Jibril kept advising me about the neighbor until I thought he was going to make him an heir." (Bukhari, Muslim). The repetition of the angel's counsel — until the Prophet ﷺ began to wonder whether neighbors would eventually inherit from each other — signals something about the weight of this obligation in the sight of Allah. A community that cannot get along with the people next door has failed at something fundamental, regardless of how much it prays and fasts.

Who Is a Neighbor?

The classical scholars debated the precise boundary of "neighbor." The most cited opinion among the Hanafi and Shafi'i scholars is that the neighbor encompasses the forty houses in every direction — a significant circle. A hadith narrated by Aisha states: "I said, O Messenger of Allah, I have two neighbors — to which of them should I give a gift? He said: To the one whose door is closest to yours." (Bukhari). This establishes a gradient: those immediately adjacent have the highest claim, with the obligation extending but diminishing as distance increases.

The Three Levels of Neighborly Relationship

Classical scholars identified three levels of neighbor, each carrying a different weight of rights. The Muslim neighbor who is also a relative carries three levels of rights: the rights of Islam (brotherhood), the rights of kinship (rahim), and the rights of neighborliness. The Muslim neighbor who is not a relative carries two layers: the rights of Islam and the rights of neighborliness. The non-Muslim neighbor carries at minimum the rights of neighborliness. The Prophet ﷺ said: "He is not a believer whose neighbor is not safe from his harm." (Bukhari). This applies without distinction of religion — the non-Muslim neighbor has a right not to be harmed.

Positive Duties to the Neighbor

Islam distinguishes between two types of obligations: kifayat — avoiding harm — and ifa' — active giving. Both apply to neighbors. The positive duties include: greeting them and responding to their greeting; visiting them when they are sick; consoling them in bereavement; congratulating them in joy; giving them gifts and sharing food; helping them in difficulty; watching their property when they are absent. The Prophet ﷺ said: "The best companions with Allah are those who are best to their companions, and the best neighbors with Allah are those who are best to their neighbors." (Tirmidhi).

Prohibitions Toward the Neighbor

The Prophet ﷺ was emphatic: "By Allah, he does not believe! By Allah, he does not believe! By Allah, he does not believe!" It was asked: "Who, O Messenger of Allah?" He said: "The one whose neighbor is not safe from his harm." (Bukhari). This triple oath is among the strongest formulations of negation in hadith. Prohibited toward the neighbor are: causing noise that disturbs their rest; blocking their light or ventilation; building in a way that harms their property; spreading their secrets; disputing their legitimate use of shared spaces.

The Prophetic Example

The Prophet ﷺ modeled excellent neighborliness throughout his life. He accepted gifts from non-Muslim neighbors and returned their gifts. He visited the sick among his neighbors regardless of their faith. When a Jewish neighbor of his died, he went to pay his respects — demonstrating that the rights of neighborliness transcend communal boundaries. The cumulative picture of prophetic teaching and practice is of a community in which the physical proximity of dwelling creates genuine bonds of responsibility — bonds that, when honored, make the neighborhood a microcosm of the broader ummah.

Neighborliness in a Fragmented World

In the modern urban context — where neighbors may live in the same building for years without knowing each other's names — the prophetic emphasis on neighborliness feels both radical and urgent. The Islamic vision of community is not the anonymous coexistence of independent households but a web of mutual awareness, care, and responsibility that makes neighborhoods genuinely livable. The Prophet ۷ said: “The best house among the Muslims is the one in which orphans are well treated; and the worst house among the Muslims is the one in which orphans are badly treated.” (Ibn Majah). By extension, the best neighborhood is one where the vulnerable are seen, the elderly are helped, the newcomer is welcomed, and the grieving are not left alone. Begin with the immediate: greet your neighbor by name, notice when they seem to be struggling, share food at significant moments, and protect their dignity in conversation with others. This is the prophetic vision of neighborhood — not an abstraction but a daily practice of recognition and care.
honesty-truthfulness-islam:
Among the character traits the Prophet ﷺ was known for before his prophethood — among the Quraysh who would eventually oppose him bitterly — was one that even his enemies never disputed: al-Amin, the Trustworthy. Long before the revelation came, the people of Mecca entrusted him with their valuables, their secrets, and their disputes. When the Prophet ﷺ climbed al-Safa before his public declaration of Islam and asked the crowd: "If I told you that an army was behind this hill about to attack you, would you believe me?" They replied: "We have never heard you say anything false." The foundation of his prophetic mission rested on a character already established in the community as impeccably truthful.

The Prophetic Statement on Sidq

The Prophet ﷺ said: "Stick to honesty (sidq), for honesty leads to righteousness (birr), and righteousness leads to Paradise. A man keeps speaking truthfully and striving to be truthful until he is recorded with Allah as a siddiq (one of great truthfulness). And beware of lying, for lying leads to wickedness (fujur), and wickedness leads to the Fire. A man keeps lying and striving to lie until he is recorded with Allah as a liar." (Bukhari, Muslim). This hadith maps two trajectories. The first begins with sidq, accumulates into righteousness, and reaches Paradise — and the person who walks it earns the title siddiq, the highest rank after prophethood, shared by Abu Bakr al-Siddiq. The second begins with lying, accumulates into wickedness, and reaches the Fire.

Sidq in Speech

The most immediate domain of honesty is speech. The Quran commands: "O you who have believed, fear Allah and be with the truthful." (9:119). The Prophet ﷺ catalogued the forms of prohibited lying with precision: lying in jokes, lying to make others laugh, lying to placate a spouse or parent, lying to gain a worldly advantage, and the most severely condemned — lying about the Prophet ﷺ himself. "Whoever lies about me intentionally, let him take his seat in the Fire." (Bukhari). The believer understands that their word is a trust — when they say something, others act on it, build on it, and trust based upon it.

Sidq in Character — Consistency Between Inside and Outside

The deeper dimension of sidq goes beyond speech to the alignment between one's inner reality and outer presentation. A person is truthful in character when they are the same in public and private — when their deeds match their words, when their claims match their actual beliefs. The Prophet ﷺ warned against the worst form of character-dishonesty: "The most hated of people to Allah is the one who argues most intensely and who is double-faced: he comes to one group with one face and to another group with another face." (Bukhari).

Sidq in Business and Dealings

The Prophet ﷺ was emphatic about honesty in commercial dealings. "The merchant who is honest and trustworthy is with the prophets, the truthful (siddiqeen), and the martyrs." (Tirmidhi). Conversely: "The seller and the buyer have the right of khiyar (option) until they part. If both of them are truthful and describe their goods, their transaction will be blessed; but if they lie and conceal defects, the blessing of their transaction will be erased." (Bukhari). Honesty literally brings barakah; lying removes it.

Building a Life of Sidq

The Prophet ﷺ gave a practical path to building truthfulness: one commits to never lying — not even in a joke, not even to avoid an awkward situation — and maintains that commitment with enough consistency that it becomes character. This requires the courage to say "I don't know" when one doesn't know, to admit a mistake when one makes one, to deliver bad news when that is the truth, and to disagree openly rather than agree falsely. And it requires remembering, in those moments, that Allah hears the private word as clearly as the public one — and that the record with Allah is the only record that ultimately matters.

Truthfulness in the Age of Information

The prophetic imperative toward sidq has never been more relevant than in an era of social media, viral misinformation, and algorithmic amplification of falsehood. The Prophet ۷ said: “It is enough of a lie for a person to narrate everything he hears.” (Muslim). At a time when sharing unverified information is effortless and instantaneous, this hadith is a direct prohibition on the casual forwarding of news, videos, and claims without verification. The Quran itself established the principle: “O you who have believed, if a wicked person comes to you with information, investigate, lest you harm a people out of ignorance.” (49:6). The believer's standard is not "I think this might be true" or "I saw it shared widely" — it is personal verification or trusted, traceable sources. In practical terms, this means pausing before forwarding, checking before sharing, and being willing to issue corrections when something one shared turns out to be false.
backbiting-gheebah:
The Quran does not often use graphic imagery for sins — but for gheebah, it does: "O you who have believed, avoid much negative assumption. Indeed, some assumption is sin. And do not spy, and do not backbite one another. Would one of you like to eat the flesh of his dead brother? You would detest it. And fear Allah — indeed, Allah is Accepting of repentance and Merciful." (49:12). Eating the flesh of a dead brother — the image was chosen deliberately to produce revulsion, to make the listener feel in their gut what gheebah actually is before their mind rationalizes it away. The Prophet ﷺ reinforced this when asked to define it: "Mentioning your brother in a way he would dislike." He was then asked: "What if what I say about him is true?" He replied: "If what you say is true, you have committed gheebah. If it is false, you have slandered him (buhtan)." (Muslim).

What Constitutes Gheebah

Gheebah is the mention of a real fault of a real person — something that is true — in their absence, in a context where they would not want it mentioned. It is distinguished from buhtan (slander), which is mentioning something false about someone. It is also distinguished from namimah (tale-carrying), which is specifically the act of carrying words between people to create discord — the Prophet ﷺ said: "The namim (tale-carrier) will not enter Paradise." (Bukhari). Gheebah can be committed in words, in writing, in gestures, in images, and even in expressions.

When Mentioning Faults Is Permitted

The classical scholars identified six categories in which mentioning someone's real faults is not only permissible but sometimes obligatory. First: Complaining to a judge or authority to seek a right or stop an injustice. Second: Seeking a fatwa — if you need to explain a situation to get religious guidance, you may mention the person involved. Third: Warning others against harm — if someone asks you about a person they intend to do business with or trust with responsibility, and that person is known to be harmful, you must warn. The Prophet ﷺ was asked about certain narrators and gave frank assessments — this is what became the science of rijal criticism in hadith. Fourth: Public figures who have public roles may be evaluated and critiqued in their public capacity. Fifth: Identifying someone by a well-known characteristic when no other description identifies them, without intent to demean. Sixth: The person who is openly sinful and speaks about their own sins publicly.

Why We Backbite — and Why We Should Stop

People backbite for several common reasons: to feel superior, to bond with others through shared criticism, to process frustration, or simply out of habit. The Prophet ﷺ said: "The Muslim is the brother of the Muslim — he does not oppress him, does not abandon him, and does not despise him. The honor of the Muslim is sacred to the other Muslim: his blood, his wealth, and his honor." (Muslim). Gheebah is a violation of that sacredness.

Repairing the Harm and Seeking Forgiveness

A question the scholars debated is whether the one who committed gheebah must inform the person they backbit in order for their repentance to be complete. The majority position, including that of Imam al-Nawawi and Ibn Taymiyyah, is that informing the person is not required if doing so would cause more harm than it resolves — and in most cases, it would. Instead, sincere repentance from gheebah involves: remorse, stopping the behavior, making du'a for the person who was wronged, and defending their honor in future conversations when they are spoken ill of.

Building a Culture of Protected Honor

The Islamic vision is not merely of individuals who refrain from gheebah but of a community in which people's honor is actively protected. The Prophet ۷ said: “Whoever defends his brother's honor in his absence, Allah will protect his face from the Fire on the Day of Resurrection.” (Tirmidhi, authenticated). This hadith introduces a positive obligation: not just refraining from backbiting oneself, but speaking up when others are being backbitten in one's presence. The person who sits silently while gheebah takes place, nodding along or smiling at the criticism, shares in the sin. The prophetically modeled response is to change the subject, to gently redirect, or — if the situation calls for it — to speak in defense of the absent person. Building such a culture requires courage as well as sincerity — the courage to be the one who says, "Let's talk about something else."
haya-modesty-shyness:
The Prophet ﷺ said: "Haya' is part of Iman." (Bukhari, Muslim). In another narration: "Haya' and Iman are companions — when one of them is lifted, the other follows." (al-Hakim). And in a statement that captures the entire prophetic tradition: "Every religion has a defining character, and the character of Islam is haya'." (Ibn Majah). The concept of haya' is often translated as "modesty" or "shyness," but neither captures its full range. Haya' is a comprehensive moral and spiritual quality — an inner sensitivity to what is beautiful and what is shameful — that shapes how the believer relates to Allah, to other people, and to themselves.

Inborn and Acquired Haya'

Scholars distinguished two types of haya'. The first is fitri haya' — the natural, inborn sense of modesty and shame that Allah placed in the human being as part of the original creation. This is why human beings across all cultures cover their private parts and feel embarrassment when exposed. The story of Adam and Eve after eating from the tree — when they immediately reached for leaves to cover themselves — is taken as evidence that haya' is a primordial human trait. The second type is iktisabi haya' — acquired haya', the result of faith, knowledge, and spiritual discipline. Ibn al-Qayyim wrote that haya' before Allah — feeling genuine shame at exposing oneself to Allah's gaze in a state of sin — is the highest form of haya'.

Haya' Toward Allah

The Prophet ﷺ said: "Have haya' before Allah as is His due." The Companions asked: "O Messenger of Allah, we do have haya' before Allah." He replied: "That is not what I mean. Having true haya' before Allah means guarding the head and what it contains, guarding the stomach and what it encompasses, and remembering death and decomposition. Whoever desires the akhirah abandons the adornments of this world." (Tirmidhi). Haya' before Allah is thus concretely expressed: in the guarding of the mind (thoughts, knowledge, intentions), the guarding of the stomach (what one earns and what one eats), and the constant awareness of mortality.

Haya' Toward People

The social dimension of haya' shapes how the believer appears and behaves in community. In dress, haya' produces the covering of the awrah — not as an imposition but as an expression of the believer's own sense of dignity. In speech, haya' prevents vulgarity, crude humor, and the discussion of private matters in inappropriate contexts. In behavior, haya' produces restraint — the believer does not demand attention, does not force themselves into spaces where they are not needed. The Prophet ﷺ was described by those who knew him as having more haya' than a virgin girl behind her veil — and yet he was a decisive leader who spoke truth plainly and never avoided difficult conversations.

Haya' Toward Oneself

Perhaps the most neglected dimension of haya' is toward oneself — the sense of shame a person feels in their own private space about behavior that only they and Allah see. The Prophet ﷺ advised: "Be modest before Allah as you would be modest before a righteous man from your people." The believer who has internalized haya' toward Allah does not behave differently in private than in public — because the audience is always present.

Haya' and Courage

A common misunderstanding is that haya' leads to passivity. The prophetic model refutes this entirely. Umm Sulaym asked the Prophet ﷺ about a woman's ritual bath after a wet dream — a sensitive question in a culture where such things were not easily discussed. The Prophet ﷺ praised her for asking: "May Allah not be shy about the truth." (Bukhari). True haya' gives one the courage to do what is right and the restraint to avoid what is shameful. These are not opposites — they are the two wings of moral character.

Haya' in the Contemporary Context

Perhaps no quality of Islamic character is more countercultural in the contemporary West than haya'. A culture saturated with exhibitionism — from social media self-display to the normalization of explicit content — runs directly against the grain of this prophetic virtue. Yet the data on well-being increasingly supports what Islamic tradition has always held: that the erosion of modesty correlates with the erosion of dignity, relational depth, and psychological security. The believer who maintains haya' in the face of this cultural pressure is not backward or repressed — they are the one who has chosen the deeper satisfaction of self-possession over the shallow relief of constant self-disclosure. The Prophet ۷ said: “Every religion has a character, and the character of Islam is haya'.” (Ibn Majah). Haya' is Islam's distinctive contribution to the question of how human beings should present themselves to the world — with dignity, with restraint, and with the awareness that the most important audience is always Allah.
silah-ar-rahim-kinship-ties:
The word rahim in Arabic — the womb — is etymologically connected to the word rahmah, mercy. Allah named the womb after the quality of mercy that flows through it, and He made the maintenance of kinship ties a direct reflection of His own mercy. The Prophet ﷺ transmitted a hadith qudsi: "Allah created creation, and when He had finished, the womb stood up and said: 'This is the standing of one who seeks refuge in You from being cut off.' Allah said: 'Yes — would you not be pleased that I join whoever joins you, and cut off whoever cuts you off?' It said: 'Of course.' Allah said: 'Then that is yours.'" (Bukhari, Muslim). Maintaining kinship ties is thus alignment with a divine covenant made at the moment of creation.

The Quranic Command

The Quran commands silah al-rahim in terms that connect it directly to taqwa: "O mankind, fear your Lord, who created you from one soul and created from it its mate and dispersed from both of them many men and women. And fear Allah, through Whom you ask one another, and the wombs (al-arham). Indeed Allah is ever, over you, an Observer." (4:1). The reference to "asking by the wombs" shows how central the concept of rahim was in Arab culture. The Quran takes this cultural instinct and grounds it in divine taqwa and divine observation.

Who Counts as Rahim?

Scholars defined the rahim as those with whom one shares a direct blood relationship — both from the paternal and maternal sides. This includes parents, children, siblings, grandparents, grandchildren, aunts, uncles, and cousins. The obligation is not uniform: the closer the relationship, the stronger the claim. The Prophet ﷺ said: "The best of charity is that which you give to your relative who bears enmity toward you." (Ahmad). The relative who is hostile does not forfeit their right to silah — in fact, maintaining ties with the difficult relative is among the highest demonstrations of this virtue.

What Silah al-Rahim Looks Like in Practice

Silah al-rahim is not merely the absence of cutting off — it is active maintenance. At minimum, it includes regular contact through visits, calls, or messages; being present at major life events; contributing to the family member's welfare in hardship. The Prophet ﷺ gave a practical scale: "The one who maintains ties is not the one who reciprocates — rather, the one who truly maintains ties is the one who does so when the kinship is severed." (Bukhari). This means that if a relative cuts off contact, the obligation to maintain silah falls more heavily, not less, on the one willing to preserve it.

The Consequences of Cutting Off Kinship

The warnings against qat' al-rahim (cutting kinship ties) are among the most severe in the prophetic tradition. "The one who cuts off the ties of kinship will not enter Paradise." (Bukhari, Muslim). In another narration: "Allah said: 'I am al-Rahman. I created the rahim and derived its name from My name. So whoever maintains it, I will maintain him; and whoever severs it, I will sever him.'" (Abu Dawud, Tirmidhi). The severing of rahim is thus a severing from Allah's mercy — not as an external punishment applied from without, but as a natural consequence of cutting off from the very source of rahmah that the rahim embodies.

When Family Is Difficult

The reality of family life is that maintaining ties with difficult relatives requires wisdom as well as sincerity. Scholars distinguish between the obligation of silah (which is to not sever ties) and the degree of engagement required. A person who is being actively harmed by a relative is not required to expose themselves to that harm repeatedly — but they are required to maintain at minimum a form of contact that prevents the relationship from qualifying as severed. Sending a periodic message, making du'a for the relative, and not responding to harm with harm are all forms of silah that fulfill the obligation even when proximity is not possible.

The Community Built on Rahim

When kinship ties are maintained, they become the building blocks of the larger community. The ummah — the Muslim community — is itself understood in the Quran as a community of brotherhood: “The believers are but brothers.” (49:10). But this broader brotherhood is supported by and grows from the more immediate bonds of rahim. A person who has learned to maintain kinship ties with difficult relatives — who has practiced the patience, humility, and generosity that silah requires — is precisely the person equipped to build the broader bonds of Muslim brotherhood. Conversely, a community in which families are fragmented by qat' al-rahim lacks the foundational bonds from which community cohesion grows. The instruction to maintain the rahim is not just about private family relationships; it is about the social fabric of the Muslim world.
amanah-trustworthiness:
Before the Quran was revealed, before the first words of prophethood descended on Mount Nur, the people of Mecca had already given Muhammad ibn Abdullah ﷺ a title that would follow him into history: al-Amin — the Trustworthy. It was not a title the Quraysh bestowed reluctantly or politically — it was the description that naturally emerged from decades of observing a man whose word was his bond, whose custody of others' property was absolute, and whose truthfulness was so consistent it became the definition of the word in Mecca. Amanah preceded the revelation. It was the soil in which prophethood was planted.

The Scope of Amanah

The word amanah — from the root meaning safety, security, and trust — carries a range far wider than simply keeping someone's property safe. The Quran uses it in its most cosmic sense when describing the divine trust (al-amanah al-kubra): "Indeed, We offered the Trust to the heavens and the earth and the mountains, and they declined to bear it and feared it; but the human being undertook to bear it. Indeed, he was unjust and ignorant." (33:72). Classical scholars interpreted this as the trust of moral responsibility — the free will, the accountability, the capacity for worship and obedience that Allah placed in the human being alone. Every act of amanah in daily life is a fulfillment of this cosmic undertaking.

Amanah in Property and Finance

The most obvious dimension of amanah is financial: keeping what is entrusted to one's care intact. The Prophet ﷺ said: "Return the trust to the one who entrusted it to you, and do not betray the one who betrayed you." (Abu Dawud, Tirmidhi). This second clause is especially demanding: even if the other party has betrayed you, your obligation of amanah toward their property or trust remains. The believer's honesty is not conditional on the other party's honesty — it is grounded in their own relationship with Allah.

Amanah in Speech and Secrets

The Prophet ﷺ established: "When a man tells you something and then turns away, it is a trust." (Abu Dawud, Tirmidhi). Confidential information shared in a conversation — whether explicitly marked as a secret or understood contextually as private — is an amanah that the believer may not betray. This has significant implications in an age of social media, where the ease of sharing makes betraying confidences effortless. The Islamic framework of speech-as-amanah means that sharing someone's private information, even without malicious intent, constitutes a betrayal of trust.

Amanah in Responsibilities

Leadership and professional responsibility are a form of amanah. The Prophet ﷺ said: "Each of you is a guardian (ra'i) and each of you is responsible for his flock. The imam is a guardian and responsible for his flock. The man is a guardian of his household and responsible for them. The woman is a guardian of her husband's household and responsible for it." (Bukhari, Muslim). The language of ra'i — shepherd — implies active, attentive care: not just not stealing from the flock but ensuring they are fed, protected, and led properly.

Amanah as a Sign of the End Times

The Prophet ﷺ identified the disappearance of amanah as one of the signs of the approaching Hour. When asked how amanah would be lost, he said: "When authority is given to those who do not deserve it." (Bukhari). In another hadith: "When you see that people no longer fulfill trusts, then wait for the Hour." A society in which amanah has collapsed is a society near its end. Conversely, building a culture of amanah — one honest transaction, one kept secret, one fulfilled responsibility at a time — is among the most significant contributions a Muslim can make.

Building the Culture of Amanah

The Prophet ۷ described the disappearance of amanah as a communal sign of decline rather than merely an individual failure. This means that the cultivation of amanah is a communal as well as a personal project. Families that model honest dealing, schools that teach the sacredness of trusts, businesses that maintain transparency, and communities that hold their leaders accountable — all contribute to a culture in which amanah is the norm rather than the exception. The Companion Abu Dharr al-Ghifari asked the Prophet ۷ for appointment to a leadership position. The Prophet ۷ replied: “You are weak, and it is a trust. On the Day of Resurrection it will be a cause of humiliation and regret except for the one who takes it rightfully and fulfills what is entrusted to him.” (Muslim). Amanah in leadership is not a competitive advantage to be listed on a resume — it is an awesome responsibility before Allah that the Muslim who understands this takes seriously, preparing for it properly, and never treating the trust of others as a personal resource to exploit.
adl-justice-islam:
Allah commands in the Quran: "Indeed, Allah commands justice (al-adl), and good conduct (al-ihsan), and giving to relatives, and He forbids immorality and bad conduct and oppression. He admonishes you that perhaps you will be reminded." (16:90). This verse — which Imam Ali is reported to have memorized as among the most comprehensive in the Quran — pairs justice with ihsan (excellence) and then contrasts both with oppression. Justice is not the ceiling of Islamic ethics but its floor. Ihsan — going beyond what is required, giving more than one's due — is what the believer aspires to after establishing justice.

Justice Even Against Oneself

The most demanding verse on justice in the Quran sets a standard that no human legal system has consistently matched: "O you who have believed, be persistently standing firm in justice, witnesses for Allah, even if it be against yourselves or parents and relatives. Whether one is rich or poor, Allah is more worthy of both. So follow not personal inclination, lest you not be just." (4:135). Justice against oneself means admitting when one is wrong, repaying a debt one hoped would be forgotten, confessing to a role in a harm that no one else knows about. Justice against parents means not covering for them when they have wronged others.

The Prophet ﷺ's Example

When the Prophet ﷺ was asked to intercede in a case involving a Makhzumi noblewoman who had stolen — and whose influential family members came to plead on her behalf — he responded with one of the most forceful statements against nepotism in religious history: "The nations before you were destroyed because when a noble person among them stole, they would leave him alone, but when a weak person stole, they would apply the punishment to him. By Allah, if Fatimah the daughter of Muhammad were to steal, I would cut off her hand." (Bukhari, Muslim). His own daughter would not be exempt from the justice he was sent to establish.

Justice in Governance

Islamic political thought makes justice the first and indispensable quality of legitimate governance. The Prophet ﷺ said: "The just ruler is one of the seven types whom Allah will shade on the Day when there is no shade but His shade." (Bukhari, Muslim). Conversely, the unjust ruler is among the most severely warned against in prophetic teaching: "Beware of oppression, for oppression will be darkness on the Day of Resurrection." (Muslim). Ibn Taymiyyah's famous statement — "Allah supports the just state even if it is of unbelievers, and does not support the unjust state even if it is of believers" — captures the Islamic understanding that justice is so fundamental that its absence cannot be compensated for by any quantity of religious practice.

Justice in Family Life

The obligation of adl extends into the most intimate spaces of family life. When a Companion came to the Prophet ﷺ saying he had given one of his sons a gift and wanted the Prophet ﷺ to witness it, the Prophet ﷺ asked: "Have you given all your sons a similar gift?" When told no, he said: "Then I will not witness it — for I do not witness injustice." (Bukhari, Muslim). The employer who pays fairly, the manager who evaluates without bias, the parent who treats children equitably — all are practicing adl at the level where most people's lives are actually lived.

Justice as a Personal Spiritual Discipline

Beyond its institutional dimensions, justice is a personal spiritual discipline. The Prophet ۷ said: “Be just with your children, be just with your children, be just with your children.” (Abu Dawud). This triple repetition — in the context of a parent asking about giving gifts to one child — shows that adl begins at home, at the level of the individual household, before it becomes the concern of courts and governments. The Muslim who is scrupulously just in private — who applies the same standard to themselves as to others, who admits when they are wrong, who gives people their due before being asked — is both fulfilling a religious obligation and cultivating the character from which all larger expressions of justice flow. “O you who have believed, be persistently standing firm for Allah, witnesses in justice, and do not let the hatred of a people prevent you from being just. Be just; that is nearer to righteousness. And fear Allah; indeed, Allah is Acquainted with what you do.” (5:8). Justice — even toward those one dislikes — is a measure of taqwa itself.
shukr-gratitude-detailed:
Allah says in the Quran: "And if you should count the favors of Allah, you could not enumerate them." (14:34). This statement is not hyperbole — it is a description of the actual ratio of divine giving to human receiving. Every breath a person takes is a gift that, if withheld, would end everything else. Every heartbeat is sustenance from Allah. Every moment of sight, of hearing, of consciousness, of safety — each is a favor that the human being did not earn and could not provide for themselves. The appropriate response to this reality is shukr: gratitude so comprehensive that it encompasses the heart, the tongue, and every limb.

The Three Levels of Shukr

The scholars of spiritual discipline described shukr as having three essential levels. The first is shukr of the heart (shukr al-qalb): the inner recognition of a blessing and the attribution of it to Allah as its source — not attributing one's health to good genes, one's wealth to personal effort, or one's security to one's own cleverness, but tracing every good to its ultimate source in the divine generosity. The second is shukr of the tongue (shukr al-lisan): expressing gratitude verbally. The Prophet ﷺ said: "Whoever is not thankful to people is not thankful to Allah." (Abu Dawud, Tirmidhi). The third is shukr of the limbs (shukr al-jawarih): using the blessings one has received in the obedience of Allah. Al-Junayd said: "Shukr is not using Allah's blessings in disobedience to Him."

The Promise of Increase

Allah makes a direct promise in the Quran: "If you are grateful, I will certainly increase you." (14:7). The verse continues: "But if you deny My favor — indeed, My punishment is severe." The promise of increase for gratitude is not conditional on what form the increase takes — it may be more of the same blessing, or different and better blessings, or an increase in contentment with what one has. The scholars noted that the most immediate increase a grateful person receives is the increase in their appreciation of existing blessings — which is itself a form of enrichment that money cannot buy.

The Human Tendency Toward Ingratitude

The Quran is frank about the human being's natural drift toward ingratitude: "And He has given you of all you asked of Him. And if you should count the favor of Allah, you could not enumerate them. Indeed, mankind is very unjust and very ungrateful." (14:34). Ibn al-Qayyim observed that the ungrateful servant uses Allah's blessings as tools of disobedience: the body that Allah sustained is used for forbidden purposes, the wealth that Allah provided is spent on the haram, the mind that Allah gave is used to rationalize sin. This is the deepest ingratitude — kufr al-ni'mah, the denial of blessing through its misuse.

Practical Gratitude in Daily Life

The Prophet ﷺ provided a daily framework for gratitude. He taught the morning supplication: "O Allah, whatever blessing I or any of Your creation received this morning is from You alone, You have no partner — to You belongs all praise and all thanks." (Abu Dawud). He taught gratitude for small things: "Whoever is not thankful for small things is not thankful for great things." (Ahmad). The practice of specific gratitude — naming actual blessings rather than expressing a vague general thanks — is especially powerful. The believer who, at the end of each day, identifies specific gifts that Allah gave them that day is building the habit of heart-gratitude that sustains them through difficulty.

Gratitude in Hardship

The most advanced form of shukr is gratitude in hardship — not for the hardship itself, but within it. The Prophet ﷺ said: "Wondrous is the affair of the believer — everything for him is good.
